---
title: "R Markdown for Scientists"
subtitle: "NIEHS 3-Hour Workshop on Posit Cloud"
author: "NIEHS Training"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  ioslides_presentation:
    widescreen: true
    css: rmd_slides_style.css
    self_contained: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
                      comment = "", fig.height = 2.8, fig.width = 6)
```

## Workshop Agenda

| Module | Topic | Time |
|--------|-------|------|
| 0–1 | Welcome + R Markdown Basics | 35 min |
| 2 | Code Chunks and Inline Code | 30 min |
| 3 | Tables and Data Display | 25 min |
| — | **Break** | 10 min |
| 4 | Visualizations and Figure Control | 25 min |
| 5 | Scientific Writing: Equations, Citations, Parameters | 20 min |
| 6 | Output Formats, Themes, Templates | 15 min |
| 7 | Capstone: Build a Complete Report | 20 min |

<div class="notes">
Welcome. By the end of this workshop you'll be able to create reproducible
reports that combine prose, code, and results — all from one file.
</div>

## What is R Markdown?

An `.Rmd` file combines three things:

1. **Narrative text** written in Markdown
2. **R code** that runs and produces results
3. **Output** — figures, tables, statistics — embedded in the document

When you **knit**, R executes the code and produces a polished report.

> Your report IS the analysis. Change the data, re-knit, everything updates.

## Why R Markdown for NIEHS Research?

| Benefit | How |
|---------|-----|
| **Reproducibility** | Report = analysis; anyone can re-run it |
| **FAIR principles** | Code, data, narrative bundled and shareable |
| **Efficiency** | Change data → re-knit → report updates automatically |
| **No copy-paste errors** | Statistics flow directly from code to text |
| **Collaboration** | Share `.Rmd` via GitHub; full transparency |

## Before We Start {.build}

Please confirm:

> - ✅ Posit Cloud account — [posit.cloud](https://posit.cloud)
> - ✅ Project open in Posit Cloud
> - ✅ Modern web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge)

Run this in the **Console** to install packages:

```r
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "kableExtra", 
                    "DT", "plotly", "broom",
                    "patchwork", "here"))
```

# Module 1: R Markdown Basics {.segue}

## The Three Parts of an .Rmd File

Every R Markdown document has:

```
---                          ← YAML header (metadata)
title: "My Report"
output: html_document
---

# Introduction              ← Markdown text (narrative)

This study examines...

```​{r}                       ← Code chunks (R code)
mean(c(1, 2, 3))
```​
```

<div class="notes">
Open a new Rmd file live (File → New → R Markdown) and point out each section.
</div>

## The YAML Header

Lives at the very top between `---` marks:

```yaml
---
title: "Dose-Response Analysis of PFAS Exposure"
subtitle: "Supplementary Methods"
author: "J. Smith, NIEHS"
date: "`r knitr::inline_expr('Sys.Date()')`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
---
```

> **YAML is indent-sensitive.** Use spaces (not tabs), exactly 2 per level.

## Key YAML Fields

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| `title` | Document title | `"PM₂.₅ Exposure Report"` |
| `subtitle` | Secondary title | `"Methods Supplement"` |
| `author` | Name(s) | `"J. Smith, NIEHS"` |
| `date` | Date or R expression | `` "`r knitr::inline_expr('Sys.Date()')`" `` |
| `output` | Format + options | `html_document`, `word_document` |

## Markdown: Text Formatting

| What you type | What you see |
|---------------|-------------|
| `**bold**` | **bold** |
| `*italic*` | *italic* |
| `***bold italic***` | ***bold italic*** |
| `` `code` `` | `code` |
| `~~strikethrough~~` | ~~strikethrough~~ |
| `superscript^2^` | superscript^2^ |
| `subscript~2~` | subscript~2~ |

## Markdown: Structure

```markdown
# Heading 1 (largest)
## Heading 2
### Heading 3

- Bullet item
  - Sub-item (indent 2 spaces)

1. Numbered item
2. Second item

> Block quote

[Link text](https://www.niehs.nih.gov)

---   (horizontal rule)
```

These headings auto-generate the **table of contents**.

## Markdown: Footnotes

```markdown
PFAS exposure is a growing concern[^1].

[^1]: See EPA PFAS Strategic Roadmap, 2021.
```

Renders as:

PFAS exposure is a growing concern[^1].

[^1]: See EPA PFAS Strategic Roadmap, 2021.

# Knitting {.segue}

## How Knitting Works

```
.Rmd file
    ↓
knitr (runs R code, inserts results)
    ↓
.md file (Markdown with output)
    ↓
Pandoc (converts to final format)
    ↓
.html / .docx / .pdf
```

**Click Knit** or press `Ctrl+Shift+K` (Cmd+Shift+K on Mac).

## Knitting Runs in a Clean Session

When you knit, R starts **fresh**:

- Objects from the Console are **NOT** available
- Every package must be loaded with `library()` in the `.Rmd`
- Every data file must be read inside the `.Rmd`

> This guarantees **reproducibility**. If it knits, anyone can reproduce it.

## Exercise 1.1: Your First R Markdown

1. **File → New File → R Markdown...**
2. Title: "My First R Markdown", your name
3. Keep default HTML output → OK
4. Click **Knit** → see the rendered output
5. Now modify:
   - Change the title
   - Add a **bold** sentence and an *italic* one
   - Add a numbered list of 3 things
   - Re-knit!

**Time: 5 minutes**
